Training parameters For IBP, across all datasets, the networks were trained using the Adam [31] algorithm with an initial learning rate of 10−3. We linearly ramp-down the value ofκbetween 1 and κﬁnal after a ﬁxed warm-up period (κﬁnal is set to both 0 or 0.5 and the best result is used). Simultaneously, we lineary ramp-up the value of ϵbetween 0 and ϵtrain (for CIFAR -10 and SVHN , we use a value of ϵtrain that is 10% higher than the desired robustness radius). MNIST is trained on a single Nvidia V100 GPU. CIFAR -10 , SVHN and IMAGE NET are trained on 32 tensor processing units (TPU) [32] with 2 workers with 16 TPU cores each. •For MNIST , we train on a single Nvidia V100 GPU for 100 epochs with batch sizes of 100. The total number of training steps is 60K. We decay the learning rate by 10×at steps 15K and 25K. We use warm-up and ramp-up durations of 2K and 10K steps, respectively. We do not use any data augmentation techniques and use full28 ×28 images without any normalization. • CIFAR -10 , we train for 3200 epochs with batch sizes of 1600 (training for 350 epochs with batch sizes of 50 reaches 71.70% veriﬁed error rate when ϵ= 8/255). The total number of training steps is 100K. We decay the learning rate by 10×at steps 60K and 90K. We use warm-up and ramp-up durations of 5K and 50K steps, respectively. During training, we add random translations and ﬂips, and normalize each image channel (using the channel statistics from the train set). •For SVHN , we train for 2200 epochs with batch sizes of 50 (training for 240 epochs with batch sizes of 50 reaches within 1% of the veriﬁed error rate). The total number of training steps is 100K. The rest of the schedule is identical to CIFAR -10. During training, we add random translations, and normalize each image channel (using the channel statistics from the train set). •For IMAGE NET, we train for 160 epochs with batch sizes of 1024. The total number of training steps is 200K. We decay the learning rate by 10 ×at steps 120K and 180K. We use warm-up and ramp-up durations of 10K and 100K steps, respectively. We use images downscaled to 64 ×64 (resampled using pixel area relation, which gives moir´e-free images). During training, we use random crops of 56 ×56 and random ﬂips. During testing, we use a central 56 ×56 crop. We also normalize each image channel (using the channel statistics from the train set). The networks trained using Wong et al. [25] were trained using the schedule and learning rate proposed by the authors. For Madry et al. [9], we used a learning rate schedule identical to IBP and, for the inner optimization, adversarial examples are generated by 7 steps of PGD with Adam [31] and a learning rate of 10−1. Note that our reported results for these two methods closely match or beat published results, giving us conﬁdence that we performed a fair comparison. Figure 5 shows how the empirical PGD accuracy (on the test set) increases as training progresses for IBP and Madry et al.. This plot shows the median performance (along with the 25th and 75th percentiles across 10 independent training processes) and conﬁrms that IBP is stable and produces consistent results. Additionally, for IBP, we clearly see the effect of ramping the value of ϵup during training (which happens between steps 2K and 12K). 0 5000 10000 15000 20000 Training step 0% 20% 40% 60% 80% 100% Test accuracy IBP (PGD ε = 0.3) IBP (nominal ε = 0) Madry et al. (PGD ε = 0. 3) Madry et al. (nominal ε = 0) Figure 5: Median evolution of the nominal (no attacks) and empirical PGD accuracy (under perturbations of ϵ= 0.3) as training progresses for 10 independently trained large models on MNIST . The shaded areas indicate the 25th and 75th percentiles.B. Convolutional ﬁlters Figure 6 shows the ﬁrst layer convolutional ﬁlters resulting from training a small robust model on MNIST against a perturbation radius of ϵ= 0.1. Overall, the ﬁlters tend to be extremely sparse – at least when compared to the ﬁlters obtained by training a nominal non-robust model (this observation is consistent with [25]). We can qualitatively observe that Wong et al. produces the sparsest set of ﬁlters. Similarly, as shown in Figure 7, robust models trained on CIFAR -10 exhibit high levels of sparsity in their convolutional ﬁlters. Madry et al. seems to produce more meaningful ﬁlters, but they remain sparse compared to the non-robust model. This analysis suggests that IBP strongly limits the capacity of the underlying network. As a consequence, larger models are often preferable. Larger models, however, can lead to the explosion of intermediate interval bounds – and this is the main reason why values of ϵmust be carefully scheduled. Techniques that combine tighter (but slower) relaxations with IBP could be used in the initial training stages when training deeper and wider models. (a) Nominal  (b) IBP (c) Wong et al.  (d) Madry et al. Figure 6: First layer convolutional ﬁlters resulting from training a small robust model on MNIST against a perturbation radius of ϵ= 0.1 for all methods. (a) Nominal  (b) IBP (c) Wong et al.  (d) Madry et al. Figure 7: First layer convolutional ﬁlters resulting from training a small robust model on CIFAR -10 against a perturbation radius of ϵ= 2/255 for all methods.C. Ablation study Table 5 reports the performance of each model for different training methods onMNIST with ϵ= 0.4 (averaged over 10 independent training processes). We chose MNIST for ease of experimentation and a large perturbation radius to stress the limit of IBP. The table shows the effect of the elision of the last layer and the cross-entropy loss independently. We do not report results without the schedule on ϵas all models without the schedule are unable to train (i.e., reaching only 11.35% accuracy at best). We observe that all components contribute to obtaining a good ﬁnal veriﬁed error rate. This is particularly visible for the small model, where adding elision improves the bound by 3.88% (12.9% relative improvement) when using a softplus loss. Using cross-entropy (instead of softplus) also results in a signiﬁcant improvement of 4.42% (14.7% relative improvement). Ultimately, the combination of cross-entropy and elision shows an improvement of 5.15%. Although present for larger models, these improvements are less visible. This is another example of how models with larger capacity are able to adapt to get tight bounds. Finally, it is worth noting that elision tends to provide results that are more consistent (as shown by the 25th and 75th percentiles). D. Additional results Table 6 provides complementary results to the ones in Table 4. It includes results for each individual model architecture, as well as results from the literature (for matching model architectures). The test error corresponds to the test set error rate when there is no adversarial perturbation. For models that we trained ourselves, the PGD error rate is calculated using 200 iterations of PGD and 10 random restarts. The veriﬁed bound on the error rate is obtained using the MIP/LP cascade described in Section 4. We observe that with the exception of CIFAR -10 with ϵ= 2/255, IBP outperforms all other models by a signiﬁcant margin (even for equivalent model sizes). For CIFAR -10 with ϵ = 2/255, it was important to increase the model size to obtain competitive results. However, since IBP is cheap to run (i.e., only two additional passes), we can afford to run it on much larger models. E. Runtime When training the small network on MNIST with a Titan Xp GPU (where standard training takes 1.5 seconds per epoch), IBP only takes 3.5 seconds per epoch compared to 8.5 seconds for Madry et al. and 2 minutes for Wong et al. (using random projection of 50 dimensions). Indeed, as detailed in Section 3 (under the paragraph “interval bound propagation”), IBP creates only two additional passes through the network compared to Madry et al. for which we used seven PGD steps during training. Xiao et al.’s method takes the same amount of time as IBP as it needs to perform bound propagation too. Similarly they use a perturbation radius of 0.03 post-normalization, it is roughly equivalent to 2/255 pre-normalization. **** We conﬁrmed with Wang et al. that [ 33] use a perturbation radius of 0.0348 post-normalization, it is roughly equivalent to 2/255 pre-normalization.F. When Projected Gradient Descent is not enough For a given example in MNIST , this section compares the worst-case attack found by PGD with the one found using a complete solver. The underlying model is a medium sized network trained using IBP with ϵ= 0.1. The nominal image, visible in Figure 8a, has the label “eight”, and corresponds to the 1365th image of the test set. The worst-case perturbation of size ϵ= 0.1 found using 200 PGD iterations and 10 random restarts is shown in Figure 8b. In this particular case, the robust network is still able to successfully classify the attack as an “eight”. Without any veriﬁable proof, we could wrongly assume that our network is robust to ℓ∞perturbation on that image. However, when running a complete solver (using a MIP formulation), we are able to ﬁnd a counter-example that successfully induces the model to misclassify the “eight” as a “two” (as shown in Figure 8c). Label: 8 - Prediction: 8 - Adversarial: 8 (a) Nominal image correctly classiﬁed as an “eight” Label: 8 - Prediction: 8 - Adversarial: 8 (b) Worst attack found using PGD still classiﬁed as an “eight” Label: 8 - Prediction: 8 - Secondary: 2 (c) Actual worst attack found using a MIP solver incorrectly classiﬁed as a “two” Figure 8: Attacks of size ϵ= 0.1 found on the 1365th image of the MNIST test set. For (b) and (c), the left pane shows the adversarial image, while the right pane shows the perturbation rescaled for clarity. Figure 9 shows the untargeted adversarial loss (optimized by PGD) around the nominal image. In these loss landscapes, we vary the input along a linear space deﬁned by the worse perturbations found by PGD and the MIP solver. The uand vaxes represent the magnitude of the perturbation added in each of these directions respectively and the zaxis represents the loss. Typical cases where PGD is not optimal are often a combination of two factors that are qualitatively visible in this ﬁgure: •We can observe that the MIP attack only exists in a corner of the projected ℓ∞-bounded ball around the nominal image. Indeed, since PGD is a gradient-based method, it relies on taking gradient steps of a given magnitude (that depends on the learning rate) at each iteration. That is, unless we allow the learning rate to decay to a sufﬁciently small value, the reprojection on the norm-bounded ball at each iteration will force the PGD solution to bounce between the edges of that ball without hitting its corner. •The second, more subtle, effect concerns the gradient direction. Figure 9b, which shows a top-view of the loss landscape, indicates that a large portion of ℓ∞ball around the nominal image pushes the PGD solution towards the right (rather than the bottom). In other words, gradients cannot always be trusted to point towards the true worst-case attack.u 0.15 0.10 0.05 0.000.050.100.150.20 v 0.15 0.10 0.05 0.00 0.05 0.10 0.15 0.20 loss 2 0 2 4 6 8 10 2 8 (a) 0.10  0.05  0.00 0.05 0.10 u 0.10 0.05 0.00 0.05 0.10 v 1.8 0.0 1.8 3.6 5.4 7.2 9.0 loss (b) Figure 9: Loss landscapes around the nominal image of an “eight”. It is generated by varying the input to the model, starting from the original input image toward either the worst attack found using PGD (udirection) or the one found using a complete solver (vdirection). In (a), the z axis represents the loss and the orange and blue colors on the surface represent the classiﬁcation predicted by the model. We observe that while the PGD attack (blue dot) is correctly classiﬁed as an “eight”, the MIP attack (red dot) is misclassiﬁed as a “two”. Panel (b) shows a top-view of the same landscape with the decision boundary in black. For both panels, the diamond-shape represents the projected ℓ∞ ball of size ϵ= 0.1 around the nominal image.

reference_research_study_list.title: Semantic adversarial examples
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
Deep neural networks are known to be vulnerable to adversarial examples,
i.e., images that are maliciously perturbed to fool the model. Generating
adversarial examples has been mostly limited to finding small perturbations
that maximize the model prediction error. Such images, however, contain
artificial perturbations that make them somewhat distinguishable from natural
images. This property is used by several defense methods to counter adversarial
examples by applying denoising filters or training the model to be robust to
small perturbations.
  In this paper, we introduce a new class of adversarial examples, namely
"Semantic Adversarial Examples," as images that are arbitrarily perturbed to
fool the model, but in such a way that the modified image semantically
represents the same object as the original image. We formulate the problem of
generating such images as a constrained optimization problem and develop an
adversarial transformation based on the shape bias property of human cognitive
system. In our method, we generate adversarial images by first converting the
RGB image into the HSV (Hue, Saturation and Value) color space and then
randomly shifting the Hue and Saturation components, while keeping the Value
component the same. Our experimental results on CIFAR10 dataset show that the
accuracy of VGG16 network on adversarial color-shifted images is 5.7%.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: Semantic Adversarial Examples Hossein Hosseini Radha Poovendran Network Security Lab (NSL) Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Washington, Seattle, W A dog frog ship bird cat horse ship automobile airplane automobile airplane automobile bird cat deer dog frog horse ship truckOriginal Labels: Predicted Labels: Original  Images Adversarial  Images Figure 1: Samples of CIFAR10 original images (top) and semantic adversarial examples (bottom) on VGG16 network. Adversarial images are generated by converting original images into the HSV color space and randomly shifting the Hue and Saturation components, while keeping Value the same. All images in ﬁrst row are correctly classiﬁed by the model. Abstract Deep neural networks are known to be vulnerable to ad- versarial examples, i.e., images that are maliciously per- turbed to fool the model. Generating adversarial examples has been mostly limited to ﬁnding small perturbations that maximize the model prediction error. Such images, how- ever, contain artiﬁcial perturbations that make them some- what distinguishable from natural images. This property is used by several defense methods to counter adversarial ex- amples by applying denoising ﬁlters or training the model to be robust to small perturbations. In this paper, we introduce a new class of adversarial ex- amples, namely “Semantic Adversarial Examples, ” as im- ages that are arbitrarily perturbed to fool the model, but in such a way that the modiﬁed image semantically represents the same object as the original image. We formulate the problem of generating such images as a constrained opti- mization problem and develop an adversarial transforma- tion based on the shape bias property of human cognitive system. In our method, we generate adversarial images by ﬁrst converting the RGB image into the HSV (Hue, Satura- tion and Value) color space and then randomly shifting the Hue and Saturation components, while keeping the Value component the same. Our experimental results on CIFAR10 dataset show that the accuracy of VGG16 network on ad- versarial color-shifted images is 5.7%. 1. Introduction Image classiﬁers are vulnerable to adversarial inputs, i.e., it is possible to carefully modify an image such that the model will classify it into a wrong label, while a human observer perceives the original object [1, 2]. The existence of adversarial examples is intriguing from learning perspec- tive, since models that outperform human in classifying nat- ural images can be so easily fooled by adding a hardly vis- ible perturbation. Also from the security perspective, such attacks pose a major threat, as machine learning systems are being increasingly integrated into critical and security- sensitive applications, such as autonomous cars, medical di- agnosis, and banking. Generating adversarial examples has been mostly limited to ﬁnding small perturbations that maximize the model pre- diction error [3–6]. Such modiﬁed images, however, con- tain artiﬁcial perturbations that make them somewhat dis- tinguishable from natural images. This property is used by several defense methods to make deep learning models ro- bust against small perturbations [7, 8] or to map the per- turbed image back into the space of natural images by ap- plying preprocessing ﬁlters [9, 10]. In practice, however, the adversary may not be con- strained with slightly modifying the image. That is, the ad- versary may perturb the image a lot, but in such a way that the modiﬁed image semantically represents the same object as the original image (because otherwise, we cannot expect 1 arXiv:1804.00499v1  [cs.CV]  16 Mar 2018the model to classify it correctly). To construct such images, we need to identify the types of transformations that human vision is invariant to and investigate how do start-of-the-art deep learning models compare to humans. One property of human cognitive system is the “shape bias”, i.e., when assigning a name to new items, humans weight shape more heavily than they do other dimensions of perceptual similarity, such as size or texture [11]. Convo- lutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are similarly designed to take into account the spatial structure of image data. In fact, models trained on ImageNet are shown to display shape bias as well [12]. In this paper, we make the following contributions. •We introduce a new class of adversarial examples, namely semantic adversarial examples, as images that are arbitrarily perturbed to fool the model, but seman- tically represent the original objects. We formulate the problem as a constrained optimization problem, re- quiring the modiﬁed image to be smooth and natural- looking, so as to be undetectable by current defense methods. •We propose a method for generating semantic adver- sarial images, based on the shape bias property of hu- man cognitive system. In our method, we ﬁrst convert the image from RGB into the HSV color space, com- posed of Hue, Saturation and Value color channels. We then randomly shift the hue and saturation compo- nents, while keeping the value the same. This approach generates images that contain the original object with different colors and colorfulness. •We perform the experiments on CIFAR10 dataset and VGG16 network. The results show that the model accuracy on adversarial color-shifted images is 5.7%. Figure 1 shows samples of CIFAR10 original images and their corresponding modiﬁed images that fool the VGG16 model. As can be seen, modiﬁed images represent the same object as original images. We also apply the attack on the state-of-the-art robust model against adversarial examples [8] and show that the model accuracy drops to 8.6% when tested on adversarial color-shifted images. The code for gener- ating adversarial color-shifted images is available at https://github.com/HosseinHosseini/ Semantic-Adversarial-Examples. The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents the problem of generating semantic adversarial ex- amples. In section3, we ﬁrst provide a background on shape bias property and HSV color space and then propose a method for generating adversarial color-shifted images. Section 4 presents the experimental results. Section 5 re- views the related works and Section 6 concludes the paper. 2. Problem Statement 2.1. Adversarial Examples In this paper, we consider the misclassiﬁcation attack. Current techniques for generating adversarial examples try to ﬁnd a perturbation that maximizes the network prediction error. Let F be the machine learning classiﬁer and xbe the given image. The adversarial perturbation is typically found by solving the following optimization problem [2]: min ∥δ∥ (1) s.t. F(x+ δ) ̸= F(x). The added perturbation in adversarial examples is usu- ally small and, hence, the modiﬁed image is likely to belong to the same class as the original image. The image, however, does contain an artiﬁcial perturbation that makes it some- what distinguishable from natural images. This property is used by several defense methods to counter adversarial ex- amples by explicitly applying denoising operations [9, 10] or training the model to do so implicitly [7, 8]. 2.2. Semantic Adversarial Examples In practice, the adversary may not be constrained with slightly modifying the image. That is, the image can be modiﬁed by any transformation, conditioned that the trans- formation preservers the semantics of the image. Let Ω be the human vision system. The problem of generating se- mantic adversarial examples is stated as follows: ﬁnd x∗ (2) s.t. Ω(x∗) = Ω(x) and F(x∗) ̸= F(x). The problem (2) can be seen as mapping any given image into the space of natural images that are misclassiﬁed by the model, but contain the original object. In this sense, wrongly-classiﬁed clean images are adversarial examples with zero perturbation. Identifying and studying adversarial transformations is important from the learning perspective, since it helps to investigate how the model compares to human visual system and also to analyze the model generalization performance. Moreover, such adversarial transformations will be able to evade state-of-the-art defense methods that try to reverse the added perturbation. Therefore, it is important also from the security perspective to identify the attack space and develop more robust defense mechanisms. 3. Proposed Method In this section, we ﬁrst review the shape bias property of human cognitive system. We then provide a background on HSV color space and ﬁnally propose a method for gener- ating semantic adversarial examples by shifting the image color components.3.1. Shape Bias Property of Human Cognitive Sys- tem To construct semantic adversarial examples, we need to identify the properties of human vision system. One such property is the “shape bias,” stating that humans prefer to categorize objects according to their shape rather than color [11]. Therefore, we expect machine learning models to also correctly classify images that contain the original object with different colors. In the following, we propose a method to generate such images. 3.2. HSV Color Space HSV (Hue, Saturation and Value) is an alternative to RGB (red, green and blue) color space and is known to more closely represent the way human vision perceives color [13]. The hue channel corresponds to the color’s po- sition on the color wheel. As hue increases from 0 to 1, the color transitions from red to orange, yellow, green, cyan, blue, magenta, and ﬁnally back to red. Saturation measures the colorfulness, i.e., setting saturation to 0 yields a gray- scale image and increasing it to 1 generates the most col- orful image with same colors. Value shows the brightness, which is maximum value of red, green and blue compo- nents. 3.3. Color-Shifted Images as Semantic Adversarial Examples HSV can be seen as a color space, in which color com- ponents (hue and saturation) are decoupled from the object structure (brightness). Therefore, by changing the hue and saturation components and keeping the value the same, we can generate images that contain the original object with different color and colorfulness. Let xH,xS and xV re- spectively denote the hue, saturation and value components of image x. The problem of generating semantic adversar- ial examples by changing the image color can be stated as follows: ﬁnd x∗ (3) s.t. x∗ V = xV and F(x∗) ̸= F(x). For solving (3), we can generate random images, set their value component to xV and then choose the ones that are misclassiﬁed by the model. Equally, we can start from the given image and randomly perturb the hue and saturation components in such a way that the modiﬁed image can fool the model. These methods, however, will generate images with visible noise. In order to generate smooth and natural-looking images, we shift the hue and saturation components of all pixels by the same amount. This approach generates an image where all pixels are equally colored if the shift in saturation is posi- tive or decolored if the shift is negative. The color of all pix- Hue Saturation Figure 2: Illustration of shifting color components (hue and saturation) in HSV color space on a sample image of CI- FAR10 dataset. The center image is the original one. Shift- ing hue and saturation components changes the color and colorfulness, respectively. As can be seen, the original ob- ject is recognizable in all images. els (the hue component) is also shifted by a ﬁxed amount. We call such images as color-shifted images. We also note that signiﬁcantly decreasing or increasing the saturation component generates gray-scale or too color- ful images, respectively, and hence causes the image to be less like a natural color image. To generate better looking images, we add a requirement that the saturation compo- nent be minimally shifted. Figure 2 shows a sample image of CIFAR10 dataset and several color-shifted images. The center image is the original one. As can be seen, images differ in color and colorfulness across variations in hue and saturation components, respectively. Also, while the origi- nal object is recognizable in all images, images closer to the middle column look more like natural color images. Let δH and δS denote the shifts in hue and saturation components, respectively. Adversarial color-shifted images can be generated by solving the following problem: min |δS| (4) s.t.    x∗ H = (xH + δH) mod 1 x∗ S = clip(xS + δS,0,1) x∗ V = xV and F(x∗) ̸= F(x). Note that δH and δS are scalars. The color in hue compo- nent changes in a circle, i.e., hue of 1 is equal to hue of 0. Hence, we compute the modulo of hue component with1 tomap it to [0,1]. The saturation component, however, should be clipped to the interval of [0,1]. 3.4. Algorithm To solve (4), we do a random search over parametersδH and δS, and obtain the modiﬁed image as described in ﬁrst constraint of (4). We continue generating color-shifted im- ages until the modiﬁed image is misclassiﬁed by the model or the maximum number of trials, denoted byN, is reached. The shift in hue component is chosen as δH ∼ U(0,1), where U(a,b) denotes uniform distribution in [a,b]. How- ever, to ﬁnd adversarial images with smaller saturation shift, δS is chosen as δS ∼U(−i N , i N ), where iis the iteration number. That is, we start with δS = 0 and linearly in- crease the interval after each trial. Algorithm 1 describes the method. Algorithm 1Generating Adversarial Color-shifted Images 1: Input: Classiﬁer F, Image x, Maximum number of tri- als N 2: Output: Adversarial color-shifted image x∗ or ∅ 3: xH,xS,xV ←Hue, Saturation and Value components of image x, respectively. 4: x∗ ←x 5: for i= 0,...,N −1 do 6: δH ←a number uniformly chosen in [0,1] 7: δS ←a number uniformly chosen in [−i N , i N ] 8: x∗ H = (xH + δH) mod 1 9: x∗ S = clip(xS + δS,0,1) 10: if F(x∗) ̸= F(x) then 11: return x∗ 12: end if 13: end for 14: return ∅ 4. Experimental Results Experiments are performed on image dataset CIFAR10, which consists of natural color images in 10 classes of air- plane, automobile, bird, cat, deer, dog, frog, horse, ship and truck [14]. We apply the attack on the pretrained VGG16 network [15], a robust network proposed by Madry et al. [8], and the VGG16 network trained with both original and color-shifted images. In experiments, we set maximum number of trials N = 1000. Table 1 provides the results for different models. The ac- curacy of pretrained VGG16 network drops to 5.7% when tested on adversarial color-shifted images. Figure 1 shows samples of original images and their corresponding color- shifted images that are misclassiﬁed by the model. As can be seen, the original object is recognizable in all of the ad- versarial images. Figure 3 shows attack success rate versus Table 1: Accuracy of different CNNs on test data and adversarial color-shifted images. The VGG-augmented is a VGG16 network trained with both original and color- shifted images. Network Accuracy on test images Accuracy on adversarial color-shifted images Pretrained VGG16 [15] 93.6% 5.7% Madry et al. Model [8] 87.3% 8.4% VGG-augmented 89.9% 69.1% 0 200 400 600 800 1000 Number of Trials 0 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 1 Attack Success Rate Figure 3: Attack success rate versus number of trials. For more than 14% of images, the model is fooled after trying only one color-shifted image, obtained by randomly shifting only the hue component. number of trials. For more than 14% of images, the model can be fooled by trying only one modiﬁed image, obtained by randomly shifting only the hue component. The results show that although the model achieves accuracy of 93.6% on test data, it is fragile to images with maliciously-shifted color components. We also applied targeted attack on VGG16 network. In our method, the attack space is limited, since we search over only two parameters. Nevertheless, we could achieve 35.4% success rate on images that are correctly classiﬁed by model, i.e., adversarial color-shifting can change the model prediction to an average of more than three classes. Figure 4 shows sample images of CIFAR10 dataset, each with three color-shifted versions classiﬁed into different labels. The model proposed by Madry el al. yields the state-of- the-art results against adversarial examples [16], by provid- ing robustness against worse-case perturbations [8]. We ap- ply our attack also on this model to ensure that our method does not add noise-like perturbation to the image. We ob- served that the accuracy of the robust CNN is 8.4% on adversarial color-shifted images, implying that even if theairplane automobile frog ship bird airplane dog frog horse bird cat frog ship automobile bird horse truck airplane frogautomobile Figure 4: Sample images of CIFAR10 dataset, along with three color-shifted versions classiﬁed into different labels by VGG16 network. The leftmost-column shows original images. model is robust to perturbations around data points, it does not provide robustness to semantic adversarial examples. We also applied the attack on a VGG16 network which, at each epoch, is trained with original and color-shifted im- ages with δH ∼U(0,1) and δS ∼U(−1,1). The accuracy on adversarial color-shifted images is69.1%, indicating that the model shows more robustness when trained with same types of images. However, as pointed out in [17], robustness achieved by data augmentation may not be an indication that the model has learned higher level semantic features in the dataset. That is, the network is likely to be vulnerable to other types of semantic adversarial examples. We will explore other attack and defense methods in future works. 5. Related Work Over the past several years, many techniques have been proposed to generate adversarial examples by ﬁnding a small perturbation that can fool the model. Such techniques, including Fast Gradient Sign Method (FGSM) [3], Deep- Fool [4], Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) [5], and Car- lini and Wagner attacks [6], usually involve an optimization problem that solves for a perturbation that maximizes the model prediction error. In this paper, we proposed an im- age transformation that may introduce large pixel-wise per- turbation, but preserves the semantics of the image. Our proposed transformation has tunable parameters that can be searched over to generate adversarial images. It has been shown that CNNs trained with clean im- ages generalize poorly to images with reversed brightness, called negative images [18]. Compared to color-shifted im- ages, negative images are less recognizable to humans [19]. Moreover, image complementing is a ﬁxed transformation and cannot be adjusted to force the model to misclassify im- ages. In [17], Fourier ﬁltering methods are used to slightly modify images such that they contain the same high level abstractions but exhibit different surface statistical regular- ities. They showed that, in the worst case, the CNN accu- racy reduces more than 20% when tested on such images. In comparison, our method introduces large perturbation to the image and reduces model accuracy by about 90%. In [20], the authors showed that adversarial translation and rotation can fool CNNs. The rotated images, however, may look unnatural to humans, due to the black borders and the existence of tilted objects. In [21], the authors consid- ered the problem of generating natural adversarial exam- ples using generative adversarial networks. Their proposed method generates images that look natural, but may belong to a different class than the original class. Hence, such im- ages do not ﬁt into our deﬁnition of semantic adversarial ex- amples. In [22], the authors suggested that instead of small or imperceptible perturbations, the adversary may opt for more effective but noticeable perturbations, and proposed a method to create adversarial patches. Our method also generates images with large modiﬁcation, however in such a way that the perturbation is not visible, i.e., the modiﬁed image is smooth and natural-looking. 6. Conclusion In this paper, we introduced Semantic Adversarial Ex- amples as images that semantically represent the original object, but are misclassiﬁed by the model. We proposed a method for generating such images by shifting the color components of the image in HSV color space, and showed that the generated images are smooth and natural-looking. Our experimental results on CIFAR10 dataset show that the accuracy of state-of-the-art CNNs on adversarial color- shifted images is less than 10%. new_method.method:
Title: TAHES++ — Tangent-Aware Higher-Order Elliptical Smoothing with provable, spoof-resistant subspace control

Motivation
- Problem: Randomized smoothing (RS) scales to modern models but its certified L2 radius decays with ambient dimension and is vulnerable to off-manifold spoofing (Shadow Attack). First-order certification (HO-Cert) breaks the information-limited barrier but assumes isotropic geometry and thus remains blind to the data manifold (semantics vary mostly along tangents; nuisance lies largely along normals).
- Key gap: Existing certifiers either (i) ignore data geometry (RS4A, HO-Cert), (ii) rely on heavy second-order machinery that struggles to scale (CRC/curvature), or (iii) improve radii by transitivity but still reason in pixel L2 (GICR). None proactively exposes and constrains the normal (off-manifold) sensitivity that Shadow exploits.
- Idea: Fix the smoothing distribution but make it manifold-aware and anisotropic; certify in the corresponding Mahalanobis geometry with directional (tangent/normal) first-order constraints; and provably clamp normal-direction sensitivity using scalable Lipschitz upper bounds. Then, extend transitivity to the same ellipsoidal metric and train with a light regularizer that flattens the smoothed classifier off-manifold. This combination promises larger, better-calibrated, spoof-resistant certificates at CNN/ViT scale.

Methodology
High-level: Learn a single, global ellipsoidal covariance Σ aligned to the data manifold; whiten to isotropic space; run first-order HO-Cert with projected (tangent/normal) constraints; cap normal sensitivity via conservative Lipschitz bounds; expand regions via geometry-aware transitivity; and add a tiny training regularizer to reduce normal gradients. Report full ellipsoidal certificates plus scalar L2 summaries and a spoofability index.

A) Geometry and fixed anisotropic smoothing (keeps RS validity)
1) Tangent estimation and aggregation (data/model-agnostic)
- Use a small frozen generative backbone (contractive AE, diffusion decoder, or VQ-VAE). For each training image x, map to latent z and compute decoder Jacobian J=∂G/∂z.
- For each x, take top-k left singular vectors of J as local tangents; form projectors Px=UxU⊤x. Aggregate across data (or per class in an input-independent way) by averaging projectors and taking the top-k eigenvectors; call the resulting basis BT (normal basis BN completes it). This yields a single, fixed subspace split common to all inputs, preserving RS validity.

2) Ellipsoidal smoothing and whitening
- Define Σ=σT^2 BTBT⊤ + σN^2 BNBN⊤ with anisotropy β=σN/σT∈[0.3,0.7]. Keep Σ global and fixed.
- Compute L with LL⊤=Σ and whiten inputs y=L−1x. In y-space, noise is N(0,I) and HO-Cert applies unchanged; the certified set maps back to an ellipsoid {δ: ||L−1δ||2 ≤ ry} in x-space.

B) First-order certification with directional control
3) Projected-gradient HO-Cert in whitened space
- Extend HO-Cert’s low-variance estimators to obtain high-confidence intervals for gy(x) and projected gradient norms ||PT∇gy||2 and ||PN∇gy||2 using shared antithetic noise and control variates. Subspace versions are obtained by pre-multiplying noise by the corresponding projectors.
- Certificate: compute ry (ℓ2 in y); report the full ellipsoidal certificate (Σ, ry) and a conservative L2 summary rL2=ry·√λmin(Σ) for comparability.
- Directional tightening: plug the two projected-norm CIs into HO-Cert’s subspace formulas to reduce worst-case sensitivity in each subspace; this enlarges the certified set along tangent directions while not over-claiming along normals.

4) Lipschitz-backed normal clamping (spoof resistance)
- Global bound: compute a scalable network Lipschitz upper bound Lglob via ECLipsE-Fast (fallback: product of per-layer spectral norms). This is input-independent and valid network-wide.
- Local projected bound (optional): approximate the local Jacobian spectral norm restricted to PN via a conservative overbound: multiply per-layer spectral norms but only for paths that pass through PN at the input (subspace-aware spectral product); this yields Lloc,N ≥ sup||PN∇gy||2. Avoids heavy SDPs, remains an upper bound.
- Clamp: use L̂N(x)=min{CI upper bound of ||PN∇gy||2, Lglob (or Lloc,N)} in the subspace HO-Cert program. This strictly tightens the normal-direction constraint and prevents Shadow-style off-manifold inflation without changing the smoothing distribution.

C) Geometry-aware transitivity and training
5) Transitivity in the ellipsoidal metric
- Operate in y-space (Euclidean). Adapt GICR’s single/double-bubble to search for auxiliary points along learned tangent axes (principal components in BT), then union the corresponding ellipsoidal certificates (measured in ||·||2 in y or ||L−1·||2 in x). This increases coverage along semantic directions with fewer, more meaningful auxiliary points.

6) Light-touch training regularizer (TaReS)
- During standard RS training (Cohen/MACER/CRT), add λR·Eε[||PN∇ygθ(x)||2^2] with stop-gradient PN and a handful of noise samples. This biases the smoothed classifier to be flat off-manifold while preserving tangent sensitivity. No change to the fixed Σ.

D) Provability, implementation, and reporting
7) Validity and theory
- Fixed Σ keeps classical RS validity. Whitening reduces certification to HO-Cert’s isotropic case. Subspace constraints follow HO-Cert’s subspace results.
- Normal clamping uses valid Lipschitz upper bounds, so it is conservative.
- Under an m-dimensional manifold with bounded curvature, anisotropic smoothing plus subspace first-order certification yields direction-wise robustness driven by m (via ||PT∇g||) rather than ambient d; this evades RS4A’s information-limited curse by using first-order info and a non-isotropic metric.

8) Engineering plan (drop-in to HO-Cert/RS4A/GICR codebases)
- geometry/tangent.py: Jacobian SVD per sample; projector aggregation; save BT, BN.
- geometry/whiten.py: build L; y=L−1x transforms and N(0,Σ) samplers.
- hocert/subspace_grad.py: projected gradient CI estimators with shared noise.
- lipschitz/lip_proj.py: ECLipsE-Fast wrapper; subspace-aware spectral products; compute L̂N.
- transitivity/latent_search.py: tangent-axis auxiliary search and ellipsoidal unions in y-space.
- train/tares.py: PN-gradient penalty for Cohen/MACER/CRT.

9) Outputs per input
- Full ellipsoidal certificate (Σ, ry), Mahalanobis radius ry, scalar L2 summary rL2, and a spoofability index S=||PN∇gy||2 / ||PT∇gy||2 with CIs for auditing.

What’s new vs prior work
- RS4A: We keep its general-noise/whitening machinery but fix a learned, input-independent Σ aligned to the manifold and move beyond information-limited certification via first-order, directional constraints in the Mahalanobis metric.
- HO-Cert: We port first-order certification to anisotropic metrics and add provable normal clamping through Lipschitz upper bounds, explicitly separating tangent vs normal sensitivity.
- GICR: We conduct auxiliary-point search along learned tangent axes and union ellipsoids in the learned metric; this is stronger and more sample-efficient than pixel-L2 transitivity.
- Curvature/Lipschitz certs (CRC, LiPopt): We avoid heavy SDPs and only use fast, conservative Lipschitz bounds to cap normal sensitivity; our main certificate remains first-order and scalable.
- Shadow Attack: We expose off-manifold sensitivity (S-index), reduce it during training (TaReS), and cap it during certification (clamping), making spoofing substantially harder.

Expected impact
- Larger, more faithful certificates at scale: Ellipsoidal certificates aligned to semantics enlarge safe regions along tangents while remaining conservative off-manifold; scalar L2 summaries allow apples-to-apples comparisons.
- Spoof resistance and transparency: Directional clamping plus S-index and TaReS directly target Shadow-style spoofing and improve calibration of certificates.
- Practicality and generality: All components scale to CNNs/ViTs; Σ is fixed, whitening and first-order estimators are standard, and Lipschitz bounds use ECLipsE-Fast or spectral products. Works with existing RS training (Cohen/MACER/CRT) and integrates into HO-Cert/GICR pipelines.
- Broader applicability: The framework naturally extends to multi-subspace or perceptual metrics (swap BT,BN from a perceptual Jacobian), to per-dataset mixtures (via RS4A’s mixture-of-noise generalization), and to reporting direction-wise robustness for auditing and model selection.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
EXPERIMENT 1 — Core certification gains and calibration with anisotropic, tangent-aware first-order smoothing
Objective
- Quantify how TAHES++ enlarges certified regions and improves calibration versus RS/HO-Cert baselines.
- Validate reporting in the ellipsoidal (Mahalanobis) metric and L2 summary, plus show directional sensitivity control via S-index.

Datasets and models
- CIFAR-10 (50k train, 10k test), ImageNet-100 (100 classes subset; 120k train, 5k val/test).
- Models: ResNet-18, ViT-S/16. Train with standard RS (Cohen) and MACER variants.

Geometry backbone for tangents
- For CIFAR-10: train a small contractive autoencoder (CAE) with 64-dim latent (2M iters, MSE loss + 1e-3 Jacobian penalty), or use a public VQ-VAE.
- For ImageNet-100: use a pre-trained VQ-VAE or a light diffusion decoder (frozen).

Global tangent/normal split (BT, BN)
- For each of N=30k training samples (CIFAR) or N=50k (ImageNet-100):
  - Map x -> z, compute decoder Jacobian J = ∂G/∂z via batched JVPs (PyTorch autograd). If latent dim m is small (<=128), you can explicitly form J by backprop against m standard basis vectors; else estimate top-k left singular vectors via power iteration with JVPs.
  - Extract top-k left singular vectors Ux (k=64 for CIFAR-10, k=128 for ImageNet-100); form projector Px = Ux Ux^T.
- Aggregate: P̄ = (1/N) Σ Px (or class-wise then average across classes). Compute eigendecomposition of P̄ and take BT as its top-k eigenvectors. BN is the orthonormal complement in R^d.
- Sanity checks: proportion of energy along BT for random images should exceed 0.6 (using ||BT BT^T (x - recon(x))|| vs total).

Anisotropic smoothing and whitening
- Choose σT = 0.25 (CIFAR), σT = 0.5 (ImageNet-100). Anisotropy β = σN/σT ∈ {0.4, 0.6}. Set σN = β σT.
- Σ = σT^2 BT BT^T + σN^2 BN BN^T. Compute L such that L L^T = Σ via eigendecomposition; precompute L^{-1}.
- Whitening: y = L^{-1} x. Noise in y-space is N(0, I).

First-order certification with directional control (HO-Cert in y-space)
- For each test point x:
  - Draw M=8192 Gaussian samples ε ~ N(0, I) in y-space with antithetic pairs; evaluate g(y+ε) via the smoothed classifier estimator; use control variates for variance reduction.
  - Estimate mean score gap gy between top-2 classes and its CI.
  - Estimate projected gradient norms: ||PT ∇g||_2 and ||PN ∇g||_2 CIs using the same shared noise but with projected perturbations (PT ε, PN ε).
  - Compute ry (isotropic ℓ2 radius in y) using HO-Cert’s first-order formulas with subspace-sensitive tightening (plug in the two CIs for projected norms).
  - Report ellipsoidal certificate (Σ, ry) and scalar r_L2 = ry * sqrt(λ_min(Σ)).
- Lipschitz-backed normal clamping:
  - Compute network spectral norm upper bounds per layer via power iteration (20 iters) and multiply to get Lglob.
  - Optional: subspace-aware spectral product for PN paths at input (fast conservative overbound) to get Lloc,N.
  - Clamp the PN gradient CI upper bound by min(CI, Lglob) or min(CI, Lloc,N) before plugging into HO-Cert.

Baselines and ablations
- RS (Cohen) with isotropic σ matching σT.
- RS4A (general-noise RS) using the same Σ but zero first-order constraints.
- HO-Cert (isotropic) with σ=σT.
- HO-Cert + GICR (standard transitivity in pixel L2; 4 auxiliary points).
- TAHES++ variants: without clamping; with clamping; with/without TaReS regularizer (see Exp 3 for training).
- Anisotropy sweep: β ∈ {0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.7}.
- Tangent dimensionality sweep: k ∈ {32, 64, 96} for CIFAR; {64, 128, 192} for ImageNet-100.

Metrics
- Certified accuracy at fixed L2 radii r ∈ {0.25, 0.5, 0.75} (CIFAR), {0.5, 1.0, 1.5} (ImageNet-100) using r_L2 summary.
- Certified Mahalanobis accuracy at ry ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5}.
- Average and quantiles (p50/p90) of r_L2 and ry.
- S-index = ||PN ∇g|| / ||PT ∇g|| with CIs; correlation of S-index with r_L2 and with empirical attack success (from Exp 2).
- Calibration: fraction of certified points that remain robust under adversarial search constrained in Mahalanobis metric.

Expected outcomes
- TAHES++ (full) increases r_L2 by 15–40% over HO-Cert and RS4A at matched compute, with the largest gains on ViT-S/16 and along semantic classes with strong manifolds.
- Clamping reduces S-index tails (>95th percentile) and tightens calibration gap without hurting ry along BT.
- Moderate β (0.4–0.6) and k near intrinsic manifold dimension maximize area under the certified-accuracy curve.

Implementation sketch (PyTorch-like pseudo-code)
- Build BT:
  P_bar = 0
  for x in dataloader:
      z = enc(x).detach();
      # get top-k left singular vectors of J = dG/dz
      Ux = topk_left_singulars_of_decoder_jacobian(x, k)  # power-iter via JVPs
      P_bar += Ux @ Ux.T
  P_bar /= N
  eigvals, eigvecs = topk_eig(P_bar, k)
  BT = eigvecs; BN = orthonormal_complement(BT)

- Whitening and certificates per batch:
  L = eigvecs_full @ diag(sqrt(eigvals_full)) @ eigvecs_full.T  # from Σ
  Linv = inv(L)
  y = (Linv @ x_flat.T).T.reshape_as(x)
  # Monte Carlo estimators produce gy, CI_T, CI_N
  r_y = hocert_with_subspace_CIs(y, CI_T_clamped, CI_N_clamped)
  r_L2 = r_y * sqrt(lambda_min(Σ))

Compute budget
- CIFAR-10/ResNet-18: ~1x V100 GPU for 8–12 hours for geometry + certification on 10k test.
- ImageNet-100/ViT-S: 4x A100 for 24–36 hours (geometry with JVPs is the bottleneck). Certification is parallelizable.

Risk and mitigations
- If geometry is noisy, shrink k and use class-averaged P̄. Verify stability of BT across random subsets.
- If Lipschitz bounds are too loose, rely on Lglob (spectral product) and show robust gains from TaReS in Exp 3.


EXPERIMENT 2 — Spoof resistance under Shadow-style off-manifold attacks
Objective
- Demonstrate that TAHES++ resists off-manifold spoofing by clamping normal sensitivity and lowering S-index.
- Show improved alignment between certificates and empirical robustness under strong, PN-focused attacks.

Attack design (Shadow-inspired)
- We approximate the gradient of the smoothed class score g(y) wrt input using MC-EST with antithetic noise in y-space.
- Constrain updates primarily in PN by projecting each step: δ <- PN δ.
- Two attacks per sample:
  1) PN-constrained PGD (untargeted): maximize g_other(y+δ) - g_true(y+δ) with δ in ball ||δ||_2 ≤ ε_y and δ in span(PN) (enforced by projection). Steps: T=50, step=ε_y/5, restarts=5, M=512 noise samples per gradient estimate.
  2) Mixed-direction attack: 80% PN, 20% PT (to test robustness off- but near-manifold).
- Map ε_y to x-space L2 using ε_x ≈ ε_y * sqrt(λ_min(Σ)). Also test fixed ε_x budgets and convert to ε_y via Σ.

Compared methods
- RS (Cohen), HO-Cert (isotropic), RS4A.
- TAHES++ (no clamp), TAHES++ (clamp), TAHES++ (clamp + TaReS).

Protocol
- Use 1k randomly selected correctly classified test images per dataset/model.
- For each ε_y ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5}:
  - Run both attacks; record success if misclassification occurs.
  - Check whether the point was certified at radius ≥ ε_y (or its L2 summary). Count certified-yet-broken cases (calibration error under attack).
- Record S-index CIs before attack and relate to attack success per image.

Metrics
- Attack success rate (ASR) vs ε_y and ε_x.
- Certified-robust-yet-broken rate (lower is better).
- AUC of ASR vs S-index (monotonicity): higher S-index should predict vulnerability; TAHES++ should shift distribution to lower S.
- Average PN energy of adversarial perturbations: ||PN δ||_2 / ||δ||_2 (should be high for Shadow; TAHES++ should still hold).

Expected outcomes
- HO-Cert/RS/RS4A show high ASR under PN-PGD even at modest ε_y; TAHES++ with clamping reduces ASR by 20–40% absolute.
- TAHES++ with TaReS shows the lowest certified-robust-yet-broken rate, indicating improved certificate calibration.
- S-index strongly ranks vulnerability; post-TaReS, the tail of S-index shrinks and ASR drops.

Implementation sketch (attack)
- def smoothed_grad(y, label, M=512):
    eps = torch.randn(M, *y.shape, device=y.device)
    logits_plus = f(y + eps)  # base model logits
    logits_minus = f(y - eps)
    # antithetic trick and control variates can be added
    g_est = (logits_plus + logits_minus) / 2
    grad_est = autograd.grad(g_est[:, label].mean(), y, create_graph=False)[0]
    return grad_est

- for restart in range(R):
    δ = torch.zeros_like(y).normal_(0, 1e-5)
    for t in range(T):
        grad = smoothed_grad(y + δ, y_label)
        grad = PN @ grad  # project to normal space
        δ = δ + α * sign(grad)  # or normalized grad
        δ = project_to_l2_ball(δ, ε_y)  # in y-space

- Convert δ_y to x via δ_x = L δ_y and attack x directly if needed.

Risk and mitigations
- Gradient estimation variance: increase M to 1024 for ViT or apply variance reduction (same noise across steps; momentum).
- Time: Attacks can be parallelized over GPUs; reduce dataset size if needed for exploratory runs.


EXPERIMENT 3 — Geometry-aware transitivity and effect of TaReS training
Objective
- Show that auxiliary-point search along learned tangent axes in y-space improves certificates more per budget than pixel-L2 transitivity (GICR), and that TaReS further boosts gains by flattening PN gradients.

Training with TaReS (light-touch)
- Start from a standard RS or MACER training loop with fixed Σ.
- Add TaReS penalty: λR * E_ε [ ||PN ∇_y g_θ(x)||_2^2 ], with λR ∈ {1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3}, estimated with K=8 noise samples per batch. Stop-gradient through PN. Keep Σ and BT fixed.
- Train for 100–150 epochs (CIFAR), 90 epochs (ImageNet-100) with cosine LR decay.

Ellipsoidal transitivity along tangents
- Work in y-space. For each test point y0 of the true class c:
  - Let A = columns of BT (top-dominant axes). For i in {1..m}, m ∈ {2, 4, 8}:
    - Axis a_i = A[:, i]. Perform line search t ∈ R to find the furthest point y_i = y0 + t a_i that preserves class c with high MC confidence (e.g., bootstrap CI margin > τ). Binary search with 12 evals and MC M=2048 per eval.
    - Run HO-Cert at y_i to get ry_i.
  - Union rule: certified if a point x lies in union over i of ellipsoids E_i = {δ: ||δ_y||_2 ≤ ry_i} translated to y_i, plus the base ellipsoid at y0. Implementation: same as GICR’s union-of-balls check but in y-space; report r_L2 summary via max over ellipsoids of ry_i * sqrt(λ_min(Σ)).

Comparators and ablations
- GICR in pixel-L2 with m auxiliary points found by their heuristic; same MC budget.
- TAHES++ without TaReS.
- TAHES++ with TaReS (λR sweep).
- Axis selection ablations: use random BT axes vs top-eigen BT axes; also test class-conditional BT (if built) vs global.

Metrics
- Certified accuracy vs number of auxiliary points m at fixed compute budget (MC calls).
- Average incremental gain in ry and r_L2 per auxiliary point (marginal utility curves).
- Runtime per certified sample vs achieved r_L2 (efficiency frontier).
- PN gradient norm distribution before/after TaReS; S-index shift.

Expected outcomes
- Tangent-axis transitivity achieves equal or better certified accuracy than pixel-L2 GICR with 2–4x fewer auxiliary points.
- TaReS lowers PN gradients (median drop >30%), which translates into larger ry along BT and better union coverage, particularly for ViT-S.
- Calibration stays strong: certified-robust-yet-broken rate does not increase with transitivity.

Implementation sketch (transitivity)
- def find_axis_point(y0, a, M=2048, τ=0.05):
    t_lo, t_hi = 0.0, t_max
    for _ in range(12):
        t_mid = 0.5*(t_lo + t_hi)
        y_mid = y0 + t_mid * a
        conf_CI = mc_confidence_CI(y_mid, M)
        if conf_CI.lower > τ: t_lo = t_mid
        else: t_hi = t_mid
    return y0 + t_lo * a

- Auxiliary set = [y0] + [find_axis_point(y0, A[:,i]) for i in range(m)]
- Certificates = [hocert(y_i) for y_i in Auxiliary set]
- Union check: point is certified if distance in y-space to nearest center ≤ that center’s ry.

Compute budget
- Training with TaReS adds ~10–15% walltime vs RS/MACER (K=8 small). Transitivity evaluation adds ~1.5–2x over base HO-Cert depending on m.

Risk and mitigations
- If axis search is unstable, cap t_max using a validation-derived limit or add a prior based on class radius statistics.
- If TaReS hurts clean accuracy, reduce λR or apply early stopping on validation clean accuracy.

General notes for all experiments
- Reproducibility: fix RNG seeds, log Σ, BT eigenvalues, β, k, and MC seeds per experiment.
- Reporting: always include (Σ, ry), r_L2, S-index CIs. Publish code to recompute BT, Σ, and to verify ellipsoidal unions.
- Validity: Σ and BT must remain fixed after being computed on the training set; no test-time adaptation to maintain RS validity.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
EXPERIMENT 1 — Core certification gains and calibration with anisotropic, tangent-aware first-order smoothing

Goal
- Quantify certification gains and calibration of TAHES++ vs RS, RS4A, HO-Cert and HO-Cert+GICR. Report in Mahalanobis (ellipsoidal) and scalar L2 summaries. Validate directional sensitivity control (S-index) and the effect of Lipschitz-backed clamping.

Common setup (applies across Exp 1–3)
- Framework: PyTorch 2.2+ with CUDA; use torch.compile where safe; functorch (torch.func) for JVP/VJP.
- Libraries: torchvision, timm (for ViT-S/16), scikit-learn (IncrementalPCA, randomized_svd fallback), SciPy (sparse.linalg eigsh), numpy, pandas, tqdm; optional auto_LiRPA or torchlip for Lipschitz upper bounds; otherwise torch.nn.utils.parametrizations.spectral_norm for fast per-layer spectral norm upper bounds.
- Reproducibility: seed torch, numpy, cuda; deterministic cudnn if feasible; log seeds, Σ hyperparams (σT, σN, β), k, BT eigenvalues, training/val/test splits, MC seeds for cert/attacks.
- Data preprocessing: standard dataset stats; CIFAR-10 32x32; ImageNet-100 224x224 center-crop and resize. Normalize inputs before applying TAHES++ transforms. Flatten x to vectors of dim d when operating in subspace math.
- Notation: d = input dimension; k = tangent dimension; PT(v) = BT @ (BT.T @ v); PN(v) = v - PT(v). Whitening: y = (1/σT) PT(x) + (1/σN) PN(x). Noise in y-space is N(0, I).

Datasets and models
- CIFAR-10: ResNet-18, ViT-S/16 (timm). Training: standard randomized smoothing (Cohen) and MACER baselines.
- ImageNet-100: ResNet-18, ViT-S/16 (timm). Training identical policy, with stronger augmentations for ViT.

Geometry backbone (BT, BN)
- CIFAR-10: contractive autoencoder (CAE) with 64-d latent; MSE + 1e-3 Jacobian Frobenius penalty; train frozen for geometry only. Public VQ-VAE is acceptable alternative.
- ImageNet-100: pre-trained VQ-VAE or light diffusion decoder, frozen.
- For each training x, compute decoder Jacobian J = ∂G/∂z via JVPs. Extract top-k left singular vectors Ux of J (k=64 CIFAR; k=128 ImageNet-100) with power iteration on JJ^T using JVP/VJP. Form Px = Ux Ux^T.
- Aggregate projectors: approximate P̄ = E[Px] via streaming subspace iteration to avoid forming d×d. We define a linear operator on R^d: v -> (1/N) Σ Ux (Ux^T v). Use power iterations to estimate top-k eigenvectors of P̄. These are the columns of BT; BN spans the orthogonal complement.
- Sanity checks: measure energy capture of reconstruction residual in BT; stability of BT across random halves of the data (subspace angle < 10 degrees for top-16 axes is acceptable).

Anisotropic smoothing Σ and whitening
- σT: 0.25 CIFAR; 0.5 ImageNet-100. β = σN/σT in {0.4, 0.6} (sweep in ablation). Σ = σT^2 BT BT^T + σN^2 BN BN^T. Whitening is implemented implicitly via PT/PN; avoid forming Σ or L explicitly. Report λ_min(Σ) = min(σT^2, σN^2).

First-order certification with directional control (HO-Cert in y-space)
- For each test x: y = whiten(x). Draw M=8192 antithetic Gaussian samples ε ∼ N(0, I) in y-space; share ε across all estimators (ĝ, projected gradient norms) to reduce variance.
- Estimate: (a) top-2 class score gap g_gap(y); (b) projected gradient norms ||PT ∇g||2 and ||PN ∇g||2 via MC estimators with control variates and common randomness. Construct high-confidence CIs for all three using empirical Bernstein or Student-t with Bonferroni correction (0.05/3 per point), and use one-sided upper bounds for norms and one-sided lower bound for gap.
- Lipschitz clamping: compute Lglob via product of per-layer spectral norm upper bounds (power-iter 20 steps per layer). Optionally use auto_LiRPA’s linear bound modules to refine. Clamp PN norm CI as min(CI_upper, Lglob). Use clamped PN bound in the HO-Cert subspace program.
- Radius: pass g_gap lower CI and subspace norm upper CIs to the HO-Cert solver in y-space to obtain ry. Report ellipsoidal certificate (Σ, ry) and scalar r_L2 = ry * sqrt(λ_min(Σ)).

Baselines and ablations
- RS (Cohen) with isotropic σ = σT; RS4A with Σ but no first-order constraints; HO-Cert isotropic σT; HO-Cert + GICR (pixel L2 transitivity with 4 auxiliary points); TAHES++ variants: no clamp, clamp, clamp + TaReS (trained in Exp 3); β sweep in {0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.7}; k sweep in CIFAR {32, 64, 96}, ImageNet-100 {64, 128, 192}.

Metrics
- Certified accuracy at fixed L2 radii r ∈ {0.25, 0.5, 0.75} (CIFAR), {0.5, 1.0, 1.5} (IN-100) using r_L2.
- Certified Mahalanobis accuracy at ry ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5}.
- Mean/p50/p90 of r_L2 and ry.
- S-index = ||PN ∇g|| / ||PT ∇g|| with CIs; Pearson/Spearman correlation with r_L2 and with attack outcomes from Exp 2.
- Calibration (shared with Exp 2): fraction of certified points at ry ≥ ε_y or r_L2 ≥ ε_x that are broken by PN-focused attacks.

Reliability and statistical practices
- Use independent held-out subset for geometry sanity checks; BT and Σ are fixed before any test-time certification. No test-time adaptation of Σ/BT.
- CIs: Use empirical Bernstein bounds for g_gap and gradient norms with a 95% level; Bonferroni across the three quantities per input; union bound across transitivity centers if used. For S-index CI, bootstrap over shared-noise batches with 1000 resamples, bias-corrected accelerated (BCa) intervals.
- Multiple seeds (≥3) for each configuration; report mean ± 95% CI across seeds for scalar aggregates. Stratify per-class when reporting averages.

Example code snippets (PyTorch; single quotes to avoid JSON escaping)

Helper: projections and whitening

import torch

def proj_T(v, BT):
    # BT: [d, k], v: [d] or [B, d]
    if v.dim() == 1:
        return BT @ (BT.t() @ v)
    return (v @ BT) @ BT.t()

def proj_N(v, BT):
    return v - proj_T(v, BT)

def whiten_x(x_flat, BT, sigma_T, sigma_N):
    PT_x = proj_T(x_flat, BT)
    PN_x = x_flat - PT_x
    return PT_x / sigma_T + PN_x / sigma_N

def dewhiten_delta_y(delta_y, BT, sigma_T, sigma_N):
    PT_y = proj_T(delta_y, BT)
    PN_y = delta_y - PT_y
    return PT_y * sigma_T + PN_y * sigma_N

Computing per-layer spectral norms and Lglob

import torch.nn as nn

@torch.no_grad()
def spectral_norm_upper_bound(model, power_steps=20):
    bounds = []
    for mod in model.modules():
        if isinstance(mod, (nn.Linear, nn.Conv2d)):
            W = mod.weight.data.reshape(mod.weight.size(0), -1)
            u = torch.randn(W.size(0), device=W.device)
            u = u / (u.norm() + 1e-12)
            for _ in range(power_steps):
                v = W.t().matmul(u)
                v = v / (v.norm() + 1e-12)
                u = W.matmul(v)
                u = u / (u.norm() + 1e-12)
            sigma = u.dot(W.matmul(v))
            bounds.append(sigma.abs().item())
    Lglob = 1.0
    for b in bounds:
        Lglob *= b
    return Lglob

Decoder-Jacobian top-k left singulars via JVP/VJP power iteration

from torch.func import jvp, vjp

def topk_left_singulars_decoder(decoder, z, x_shape, k=64, iters=10, oversample=16):
    # We build an operator for JJ^T applying to a vector u in R^d: JJ^T u = J (J^T u)
    # JVP: J v  ;  VJP: J^T u
    def J_v(v):
        v = v.view(x_shape)
        y, jvp_out = jvp(decoder, (z,), (torch.zeros_like(z),), v)
        return jvp_out.view(-1)
    def JT_u(u):
        u = u.view(x_shape)
        y, vjp_fun = vjp(decoder, z)
        (JT_u_flat,) = vjp_fun(u)
        return JT_u_flat
    # Randomized range finder on JJ^T
    d = x_shape.numel()
    q = k + oversample
    U = torch.randn(d, q, device=z.device)
    U, _ = torch.linalg.qr(U, mode='reduced')
    for _ in range(iters):
        # U <- orth(J J^T U)
        JJtU = []
        for i in range(q):
            u = U[:, i]
            JuT = JT_u(u)
            JJt_u = J_v(JuT)
            JJtU.append(JJt_u)
        JJtU = torch.stack(JJtU, dim=1)
        U, _ = torch.linalg.qr(JJtU, mode='reduced')
    # Rayleigh-Ritz to get top-k
    B = U.t() @ JJtU
    eigvals, eigvecs = torch.linalg.eigh(B)
    idx = torch.argsort(eigvals, descending=True)[:k]
    Uk = U @ eigvecs[:, idx]
    return Uk  # columns are approx top-k left singular vectors in R^d

Streaming subspace iteration for P̄ eigenvectors (BT)

@torch.no_grad()
def compute_BT_streaming(dataloader, encoder, decoder, k=64, iters=6, sample_cap=50000):
    # linear operator v -> (1/N) sum Ux(Ux^T v)
    device = next(decoder.parameters()).device
    # Initialize BT with random orthonormal basis
    d = int(torch.prod(torch.tensor(dataloader.dataset[0][0].shape)))
    Q = torch.randn(d, k, device=device)
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(Q, mode='reduced')
    N = 0
    for _ in range(iters):
        Acc = torch.zeros_like(Q)
        seen = 0
        for x, _ in dataloader:
            if seen >= sample_cap:
                break
            B = x.size(0)
            x = x.to(device)
            z = encoder(x).detach()
            for b in range(B):
                Uk = topk_left_singulars_decoder(decoder, z[b], x[b].shape, k=k)
                # Apply projector Px to Q: Ux(Ux^T Q)
                Acc += Uk @ (Uk.t() @ Q)
            seen += B
        N = seen
        Acc /= max(N, 1)
        Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(Acc, mode='reduced')
    BT = Q
    return BT  # BN is implicit via I - BT BT^T

MC estimators for gap and projected gradient norms (antithetic + control variates)

import torch.nn.functional as F

class SmoothedModel:
    def __init__(self, base_model):
        self.f = base_model
    def logits(self, y):
        # y is whitened input in R^d reshaped to original image shape as needed
        return self.f(y)

@torch.no_grad()
def estimate_gap_and_proj_grad(y, label, BT, model, M=8192, shared_eps=None):
    # y: [B, d_flat], assume model takes images; reshape inside
    B, d = y.shape
    device = y.device
    if shared_eps is None:
        eps = torch.randn(M//2, B, d, device=device)
        eps = torch.cat([eps, -eps], dim=0)
    else:
        eps = shared_eps
    def to_img(u):
        # helper to reshape (B,d) to image shape
        return u.view(B, *input_shape)
    y_img = to_img(y)
    # Evaluate logits at y+eps
    logits = []
    grads_PT = []
    grads_PN = []
    for mchunk in torch.split(eps, 512, dim=0):
        y_plus = y + mchunk  # y-space noise
        y_plus_img = to_img(y_plus)
        lp = model.logits(y_plus_img)
        logits.append(lp)
        # gradient wrt y for the true class logit mean across noise in this chunk
        logit_true = lp[:, label]
        grad = torch.autograd.grad(logit_true.mean(), y, retain_graph=False, create_graph=False)[0]
        # project gradients
        gT = proj_T(grad, BT)
        gN = grad - gT
        grads_PT.append(gT)
        grads_PN.append(gN)
    logits = torch.stack(logits, dim=0)  # [M, B, C]
    gmean = logits.mean(dim=0)
    top2 = torch.topk(gmean, k=2, dim=1).indices
    c0 = top2[:, 0]
    c1 = top2[:, 1]
    gap = gmean[torch.arange(B), c0] - gmean[torch.arange(B), c1]
    # projected gradients aggregated across chunks (approximate MC)
    gT = torch.stack(grads_PT, dim=0).mean(dim=0)
    gN = torch.stack(grads_PN, dim=0).mean(dim=0)
    normT = gT.norm(dim=1)
    normN = gN.norm(dim=1)
    return gap, normT, normN

CIs using empirical Bernstein (per-sample)

import math

def empirical_bernstein_CI(samples, delta=0.05):
    # samples: [M]
    m = samples.mean()
    v = samples.var(unbiased=True)
    n = samples.numel()
    rad = torch.sqrt(2 * v * torch.log(torch.tensor(2/delta)) / n) + 7 * torch.log(torch.tensor(2/delta)) / (3*(n-1))
    return m - rad, m + rad

Certificate driver (wraps HO-Cert; placeholder formula if HO-Cert API is unavailable)

def hocert_radius_y(gap_lo, normT_up, normN_up):
    # Placeholder conservative radius using two-norm decomposition
    # r_y = gap_lo / sqrt(normT_up^2 + normN_up^2)
    denom = torch.sqrt(normT_up**2 + normN_up**2 + 1e-12)
    return gap_lo / (denom + 1e-12)

@torch.no_grad()
def certify_batch(x_batch, label_batch, BT, sigT, sigN, model, Lglob=None, alpha=0.05):
    # whiten
    B = x_batch.size(0)
    x_flat = x_batch.view(B, -1)
    y = whiten_x(x_flat, BT, sigT, sigN)
    # MC with shared noise
    M = 8192
    eps = torch.randn(M//2, B, y.size(1), device=y.device)
    eps = torch.cat([eps, -eps], dim=0)
    gap_samples = []
    normT_samples = []
    normN_samples = []
    # Estimate means; for CI we would store per-sample stats; here we use plug-in + safety factors
    gap, normT, normN = estimate_gap_and_proj_grad(y, label_batch, BT, model, M=M, shared_eps=eps)
    # Clamp PN with Lipschitz
    if Lglob is not None:
        normN = torch.minimum(normN, torch.full_like(normN, Lglob))
    # One-sided CIs (approx): use multiplicative margin
    ci_factor = 1.1
    gap_lo = gap / ci_factor
    normT_up = normT * ci_factor
    normN_up = normN * ci_factor
    ry = hocert_radius_y(gap_lo, normT_up, normN_up)
    r_L2 = ry * min(sigT, sigN)
    return ry, r_L2, normT, normN

Evaluation loop (metrics)

def evaluate_certificates(test_loader, BT, sigT, sigN, model, Lglob=None, radii_y=[0.5,1.0,1.5], radii_L2=None):
    if radii_L2 is None:
        radii_L2 = [r * min(sigT, sigN) for r in radii_y]
    all_ry, all_rL2, all_labels, all_preds, all_normT, all_normN = [], [], [], [], [], []
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        for x, y in test_loader:
            x = x.cuda(); y = y.cuda()
            logits = model(x)
            preds = logits.argmax(dim=1)
            mask = preds.eq(y)
            if mask.sum() == 0:
                continue
            ry, rL2, nT, nN = certify_batch(x[mask], y[mask], BT, sigT, sigN, model, Lglob)
            all_ry.append(ry.cpu()); all_rL2.append(rL2.cpu())
            all_labels.append(y[mask].cpu()); all_preds.append(preds[mask].cpu())
            all_normT.append(nT.cpu()); all_normN.append(nN.cpu())
    import torch
    all_ry = torch.cat(all_ry); all_rL2 = torch.cat(all_rL2)
    all_normT = torch.cat(all_normT); all_normN = torch.cat(all_normN)
    S_index = all_normN / (all_normT + 1e-12)
    # Compute certified accuracy at each threshold
    metrics = {}
    for r in radii_L2:
        metrics[f'cert_acc_L2_{r:.2f}'] = float((all_rL2 >= r).float().mean())
    for r in radii_y:
        metrics[f'cert_acc_ry_{r:.2f}'] = float((all_ry >= r).float().mean())
    metrics['rL2_mean'] = float(all_rL2.mean()); metrics['ry_mean'] = float(all_ry.mean())
    metrics['rL2_p50'] = float(all_rL2.quantile(0.5)); metrics['rL2_p90'] = float(all_rL2.quantile(0.9))
    metrics['ry_p50'] = float(all_ry.quantile(0.5)); metrics['ry_p90'] = float(all_ry.quantile(0.9))
    metrics['S_mean'] = float(S_index.mean()); metrics['S_p90'] = float(S_index.quantile(0.9))
    return metrics, all_ry, all_rL2, S_index

Compute plan
- CIFAR-10/ResNet-18: geometry (BT) + certification on 10k test in ~8–12 h on 1×V100 (or A10). ImageNet-100/ViT-S: 4×A100 24–36 h; geometry JVPs are the bottleneck. Certification embarrassingly parallel across images; shard test set.

Risk and mitigations
- Geometry instability: use class-wise P̄ with shrinkage (average class projectors, then average over classes). Reduce k and add ridge penalty on projector averaging. Validate by subspace angle stability.
- Loose Lglob: still improves spoof resistance via clamping; TaReS in Exp 3 further reduces PN sensitivity.


EXPERIMENT 2 — Spoof resistance under Shadow-style off-manifold attacks

Goal
- Show TAHES++ resists off-manifold spoofing: lower PN-constrained attack success, lower certified-yet-broken rate, and S-index predicts vulnerability. Reuse Exp 1 certificates to compute calibration.

Attack design (y-space; PN-focused)
- PN-PGD (untargeted): maximize margin against true class under L2 ball in y-space with δ constrained to span(PN). Project gradient and iterate with step α=ε_y/5, T=50, R=5 restarts. Use antithetic MC gradient estimator with shared noise across steps for variance reduction. Momentum 0.9 optional.
- Mixed-direction PGD: 80% PN + 20% PT component in each step: grad = 0.8*PN(grad) + 0.2*PT(grad).
- Map budgets across spaces: ε_x ≈ ε_y * sqrt(λ_min(Σ)). Also run at fixed ε_x and convert back to ε_y using Σ.

Compared methods
- RS (Cohen), RS4A (Σ), HO-Cert (isotropic), HO-Cert+GICR, TAHES++ no clamp, TAHES++ clamp, TAHES++ clamp+TaReS.

Protocol
- Sample 1000 correctly classified test images per dataset/model.
- For each ε_y ∈ {0.5, 1.0, 1.5}: run PN-PGD and Mixed-PGD. Record ASR, PN energy ||PN δ||/||δ|| for found adversarials. Determine if each point had certificate ≥ ε_y (ry) or ≥ ε_x (r_L2). Count certified-yet-broken proportion.
- Compute S-index CIs before attack and correlate with per-sample attack success; compute AUC of success vs S-index.

Metrics
- ASR vs ε_y (and ε_x), certified-yet-broken rate, PN energy of attacks, AUC of S-index as a vulnerability score.

Example code: smoothed gradient and PN-PGD in y-space

@torch.enable_grad()
def smoothed_margin_grad_y(model, y, true_label, M=512, shared_eps=None):
    # Returns gradient of margin g_other - g_true averaged over noise
    if shared_eps is None:
        eps = torch.randn(M//2, *y.shape, device=y.device)
        eps = torch.cat([eps, -eps], dim=0)
    else:
        eps = shared_eps
    y.requires_grad_(True)
    logits = []
    for mchunk in torch.split(eps, 128, dim=0):
        lp = model(y + mchunk)
        logits.append(lp)
    logits = torch.stack(logits, dim=0).mean(dim=0)
    top2 = torch.topk(logits, k=2, dim=1).indices
    c0 = top2[:,0]
    c1 = torch.where(top2[:,0].eq(true_label), top2[:,1], top2[:,0])
    margin = logits[torch.arange(y.size(0)), c1] - logits[torch.arange(y.size(0)), true_label]
    grad = torch.autograd.grad(margin.mean(), y, retain_graph=False, create_graph=False)[0]
    return grad

def project_to_l2_ball(delta, eps):
    norm = delta.view(delta.size(0), -1).norm(dim=1, keepdim=True) + 1e-12
    factor = torch.clamp(eps / norm, max=1.0)
    return delta * factor.view(-1, 1)

def pgd_pn_y(model, x, BT, sigma_T, sigma_N, y_label, eps_y, steps=50, restarts=5, step_frac=0.2, mixed=False):
    B = x.size(0)
    d = int(torch.prod(torch.tensor(x.shape[1:])))
    x_flat = x.view(B, -1)
    y0 = whiten_x(x_flat, BT, sigma_T, sigma_N)
    alpha = eps_y * step_frac
    best_delta = torch.zeros_like(y0)
    best_success = torch.zeros(B, dtype=torch.bool, device=x.device)
    for r in range(restarts):
        delta = torch.randn_like(y0)
        delta = project_to_l2_ball(delta, eps_y)
        for t in range(steps):
            y_cur = y0 + delta
            y_img = y_cur.view_as(x)
            grad = smoothed_margin_grad_y(model, y_img, y_label, M=512)
            grad = grad.view(B, -1)
            gT = proj_T(grad, BT)
            gN = grad - gT
            if mixed:
                grad_p = 0.8 * gN + 0.2 * gT
            else:
                grad_p = gN
            grad_p = grad_p / (grad_p.norm(dim=1, keepdim=True) + 1e-12)
            delta = delta + alpha * grad_p
            delta = project_to_l2_ball(delta, eps_y)
        # evaluate success
        with torch.no_grad():
            y_adv = y0 + delta
            logits = model(y_adv.view_as(x))
            pred = logits.argmax(dim=1)
            success = ~pred.eq(y_label)
            best_delta[success & ~best_success] = delta[success & ~best_success]
            best_success |= success
    return best_delta, best_success

Calibration computation (reusing Exp 1 certificates)
- Given ry per sample and adversarial ε_y used, compute certified-yet-broken = mean( (ry >= ε_y) & success ). Similarly for r_L2 vs ε_x.

Risk and mitigations
- Gradient variance: reuse shared_eps across PGD steps; increase M to 1024 for ViT; add Polyak momentum; line-search backtracking for step size on difficult points.
- Runtime: parallelize across GPUs; reduce restarts for exploratory sweeps; cache whitened y0.

Expected outcomes
- TAHES++ with clamping lowers PN-PGD ASR by 20–40% absolute vs HO-Cert/RS/RS4A at matched ε_y; certified-yet-broken drops notably; S-index distributions shift left, with strong monotonic relation to ASR.


EXPERIMENT 3 — Geometry-aware transitivity and effect of TaReS training

Goal
- Demonstrate that tangent-axis transitivity in y-space yields more gains per budget than pixel-L2 GICR, and that TaReS training further enlarges certificates and improves spoof resistance.

Training with TaReS (light-touch RS)
- Start from RS or MACER training with fixed Σ and BT. Add penalty λR * E_ε [ ||PN ∇_y g_θ(x)||_2^2 ], λR ∈ {1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3}. Estimate with K=8 noise samples per batch; stop-gradient through PN (treat BT as constant). Train CIFAR 100–150 epochs, ImageNet-100 90 epochs with cosine decay; monitor clean acc and robustness.

Code snippet: TaReS penalty

@torch.enable_grad()
def tares_penalty(model, x, BT, K=8):
    B = x.size(0)
    x_flat = x.view(B, -1)
    # whiten
    y = whiten_x(x_flat, BT, sigma_T, sigma_N).view_as(x)
    eps = torch.randn(K//2, *x.shape, device=x.device)
    eps = torch.cat([eps, -eps], dim=0)
    logits = []
    for e in torch.split(eps, 16, dim=0):
        lp = model(y + e)
        logits.append(lp)
    logits = torch.stack(logits, dim=0).mean(dim=0)
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
    # encourage confident correct classification while penalizing PN sensitivity
    y_true = probs.argmax(dim=1)
    true_scores = logits[torch.arange(B), y_true].sum()
    grad = torch.autograd.grad(true_scores, y, retain_graph=False, create_graph=False)[0].view(B, -1)
    gT = proj_T(grad, BT)
    gN = grad - gT
    penalty = (gN.norm(dim=1)**2).mean()
    return penalty

for x, y in train_loader:
    x, y = x.cuda(), y.cuda()
    logits = model(x)
    loss_ce = F.cross_entropy(logits, y)
    pen = tares_penalty(model, x, BT, K=8)
    loss = loss_ce + lambda_R * pen
    optimizer.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); optimizer.step()

Ellipsoidal transitivity along learned tangent axes (y-space)
- For each test point y0, consider axes a_i = BT[:, i], i = 1..m, with m ∈ {2,4,8}. Perform line search along each axis to find farthest point y_i = y0 + t_i a_i that keeps high MC confidence (bootstrap lower CI on true-class prob > τ, τ=0.05). Use binary search with 12 evaluations and M=2048 samples per eval; share MC seeds across steps.
- At centers {y0, y_1..y_m}, run TAHES++ HO-Cert to get radii {ry0, ry1..rym}. The union certificate is the union of ellipsoids in y-space. Report r_L2 summary as max_i ry_i * sqrt(λ_min(Σ)).

Code snippet: axis search and union-of-ellipsoids

@torch.no_grad()
def mc_true_confidence(model, y_img, M=2048):
    B = y_img.size(0)
    eps = torch.randn(M//2, *y_img.shape, device=y_img.device)
    eps = torch.cat([eps, -eps], dim=0)
    logits = []
    for e in torch.split(eps, 256, dim=0):
        logits.append(model(y_img + e))
    logits = torch.stack(logits, dim=0).mean(dim=0)
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
    conf, pred = probs.max(dim=1)
    return conf, pred

@torch.no_grad()
def find_axis_point(y0, a, model, label, t_max=3.0, M=2048, tau=0.05):
    t_lo, t_hi = 0.0, t_max
    for _ in range(12):
        t_mid = 0.5*(t_lo + t_hi)
        y_mid = y0 + t_mid * a
        conf, pred = mc_true_confidence(model, y_mid, M)
        ok = (pred == label) & (conf > tau)
        t_lo = torch.where(ok, torch.full_like(t_lo, t_mid), t_lo)
        t_hi = torch.where(ok, t_hi, torch.full_like(t_hi, t_mid))
    return y0 + t_lo * a

@torch.no_grad()
def transitivity_union_cert(x_batch, y_label, BT, sigma_T, sigma_N, model, m_axes=4):
    B = x_batch.size(0)
    x_flat = x_batch.view(B, -1)
    y0 = whiten_x(x_flat, BT, sigma_T, sigma_N).view_as(x_batch)
    axes = BT[:, :m_axes]  # [d, m]
    centers = [y0]
    for i in range(m_axes):
        a = axes[:, i].unsqueeze(0).repeat(B, 1)
        a_img = a.view_as(x_batch)
        yi = find_axis_point(y0, a_img, model, y_label)
        centers.append(yi)
    # Certify each center and take max ry per sample as summary (equivalent to nearest-center union radius lower bound)
    ry_list, rL2_list = [], []
    for yi in centers:
        ry, rL2, _, _ = certify_batch(yi, y_label, BT, sigma_T, sigma_N, model)
        ry_list.append(ry); rL2_list.append(rL2)
    ry_stack = torch.stack(ry_list, dim=0)
    rL2_stack = torch.stack(rL2_list, dim=0)
    ry_union = ry_stack.max(dim=0).values
    rL2_union = rL2_stack.max(dim=0).values
    return ry_union, rL2_union

Comparators and ablations
- Pixel-L2 GICR with m auxiliary points using their heuristic; same MC budget. TAHES++ without TaReS vs with TaReS (λR sweep). Axis selection ablation: top eigen BT axes vs random BT axes; optional class-conditional BT (if built) vs global BT.

Metrics
